problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem D ("Information & Network Science" — how information is communicated and how its speed/value changes). This team (Control #44173, O-award) decomposed the open-ended prompt into three sub-problems: (1) model how information spreads through a population and predict 2050 network characteristics; (2) filter/classify which information becomes "news"; (3) model how public opinion changes through a social-network graph. Each sub-problem got its own model, validated against named real datasets (US media circulation history, the Facebook SNAP social-graph, and the UCI Online News Popularity dataset).
models_used: SIIR information-flow model — an extended compartmental SIR ODE system with susceptible s(t), infected split into i1(t)/i2(t), recovered r(t), plus a fixed 'hot transmission node' fraction i0 representing mass media; dual contact rates lambda1 (media/hot nodes) and lambda2 (ordinary users) and immune probability mu; solved numerically in MATLAB
Aggregate media contact-rate weighting lambda1 = sum_i lambda_i * P_i over five media (newspaper, radio, TV, computer, mobile/online) built from US historical adoption data 1850-2012
Multiple regression / trend extrapolation on per-medium daily contact rates to project media mix forward and feed the SIIR model for a 2050 prediction scenario
Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural network for information filtering, framed as binary classification (shares >1400 = 'significant news')

Hybrid 'K-means + RLS' training procedure: K-means to place Gaussian hidden-unit centers (unsupervised), Recursive Least Squares to fit the linear output layer (supervised); shared Gaussian width sigma = d_max / sqrt(2K)

SN-SIR model — a degree-based (k-dependent) mean-field SIR on a complex network with separate transition probabilities P_si (contact infection), alpha (non-contact/external infection), P_ir (loss of interest -> recovered), P_sr (direct susceptible->recovered), coupled through the network-edge infection probability theta(t) = sum_k k P(k) i(k,t) / <k>
key_techniques: Disease-spread analogy: explicitly mapping information propagation onto epidemiological SIR compartments, then justifying the analogy before extending it
Introducing a named modeling primitive — the 'hot transmission node' — to capture mass media as super-spreaders, giving the model a distinguishing conceptual hook
Splitting the infected compartment (i1 from hot nodes, i2 from ordinary nodes) so media-driven vs peer-driven spread are separable

Grounding every model in a specifically named real dataset: Facebook SNAP graph (4039 nodes, 88234 edges), UCI Online News Popularity (39240 records, 27908 train / 11332 test), and US media adoption time series
Reusing the SIR backbone across two of the three sub-models for conceptual unity (information flow and public opinion both SIR-derived)
Reporting a confusion matrix with train (75.1%) and test (74.3%) accuracy and a ROC curve as classifier evidence
Extensive one-parameter-at-a-time sensitivity sweeps (lambda1, lambda2, mu, P_si, P_ir, alpha, P_sr, initial degree k0) each with its own figure and verbal interpretation
Using a forward prediction scenario (2050 vs 2012: ~25% faster propagation, cycle 60->50 days) to turn an abstract model into a concrete, quotable answer
why_it_won: "For a deliberately vague, qualitative ICM information-science prompt, this team imposed a clean three-part quantitative structure and attached each part to a recognized, named dataset rather than hand-waving. The standout move is the 'hot transmission node' extension of SIR: it is a small, intuitive, defensible modification that directly answers the 'how does media accelerate spread' question and gives judges a memorable signature idea. Reusing the SIR backbone gives coherence, while the RBF classifier shows methodological range (dynamical system + machine learning + network theory in one paper). Heavy, figure-rich sensitivity analysis and explicit validation against the Facebook graph and the news dataset signal rigor and real-world grounding. The 2050 prediction produces concrete numbers a judge can quote. The breadth, named-data validation, and the distinctive modeling hook outweigh the rough English and the loose derivations."
section_highlights: "Structure: a one-page summary that states all three models and their headline results up front; a contents page; a short Assumptions section; then one self-contained section per model each following the same Build -> Solve -> Validate -> Sensitivity rhythm; ending with explicit Strengths/Weaknesses. The summary sheet is strong — it names the model (SIIR), the technique (K-means+RLS RBF), the datasets, and the quantitative results (74.3% test accuracy, 2050 prediction) in a few lines, so a judge knows the whole arc immediately. Each model is introduced with a plain-language framing ('information filtering is essentially a classification problem') before the math, which lowers the barrier for a skimming reader. Figures are abundant and each parameter sweep is paired with a short interpretive sentence rather than left to the reader."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Poor English throughout (grammar, garbled tokens, typos) — survived because the modeling was strong, but is a clear risk factor, not a model to imitate

SIIR parameter values are physically implausible/unnormalized: lambda1=1000, lambda2=245 are used as raw 'contact rates' in an ODE where s,i,r are fractions, with no clear units or normalization, undermining the quantitative credibility of the curves
Many figures appear inconsistent or auto-extracted with garbled axes/values (e.g. tables of all-0.1 or all-1.0 rows), suggesting plots were not carefully checked

The 2050 prediction rests on naive trend extrapolation of media adoption plus hand-set parameter bumps ('we boost the number of hot transmission nodes'), so the headline '25% faster' number is suggestive, not derived
RBF classifier at ~74% test accuracy is mediocre and presented with no baseline comparison; feature list includes a leakage-prone 'number of shares' which is essentially the label
SN-SIR mean-field equations contain transcription errors (e.g. P_ir multiplies s(k,t) instead of i(k,t)) and the s+i+r=1 constraint is asserted but not reconciled with the separate P_sr/alpha terms
Sensitivity analysis is qualitative one-at-a-time only; no joint/global sensitivity, no uncertainty quantification, and several monotonicity claims read off idealized hand-drawn curves
reusable_patterns: Decompose an open ICM prompt into 3 crisp sub-questions and give each its own model, then reuse one backbone (here SIR) across sub-models for narrative unity

Take a textbook model (SIR) and add ONE named, intuitive extension (the 'hot transmission node') that directly targets the prompt's key phenomenon — a distinctive hook is worth more than a generic complex model
Frame a fuzzy task as a standard problem type to unlock known tools ('filtering = binary classification' -> RBF/ML)
Always bind each model to a specifically named public dataset (SNAP Facebook graph, UCI Online News Popularity, historical adoption tables) — naming the dataset and its size signals rigor
Adopt a fixed per-section template: Build the model -> Solve (state the solver, e.g. MATLAB ODE) -> Validate on data -> Sensitivity sweep with a figure+sentence per parameter
Turn the model into a concrete, quotable forward prediction scenario (2012 vs 2050 with percentage deltas) so judges get a memorable answer
Report classifier results as a confusion matrix + ROC + train/test accuracy split — the conventional, expected evidence package
Write the summary sheet to name model + method + dataset + headline numbers so the entire contribution is graspable in the first paragraph
End with an honest, itemized Strengths/Weaknesses list that pre-empts the obvious criticisms (region, platform, data availability)
